item.title: hm
item.content:encoded: I understand the appeal of linking this SCP to real-world historic events, but blaming the SCP for the deaths of over eleven hundred real people feels wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good and rather underrated- it creates a real sense of this unseen, largely unknown escalation, and the frailty of containment being down to human error. Everything about this creates a sense of being seconds (heh) away from disaster that nobody can perceive.

Strong +1.

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded: I, too, assumed by the writing that this was a seasoned contributor to the site. Great job! Very impressive debut!

item.title: MOAR
item.content:encoded: I really want to see more from you… I loved everything about this skip. I wish I could updoot twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fully agree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sorry ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lovely all-around. I normally object to the use of real-world events (current or historical) in Foundation writing, but this is innocuous enough that I think it's a valid exception.

One of the things I really like about the SCP Foundation is the diversity of what kinds of stories can be told with it, making use of many different lore aspects that have come to exist over time. I like this one especially because it's a big Church of the Broken God piece, but it's also, well, just kind of funny.

Admittedly I'm not sure if that's intentional but the idea that the Internet of Things is what will make Mekhane manifest is amazing to me.

+1, I hope this is the first of many SCPs from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great on so many levels. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but I didn't find the article itself to be that interesting. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really fantastic. It blends together Foundation lore, real computing standards and current events very well. The tone and pacing are both good, and shows a solid understanding of both the relevant anomalies and the technology.

I really like when articles fit in with real happenings so well. Hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

Responses:

The last paragraph of the description is really awkwardly phrased…

[Note on "Mekhane" vs "WAN" vs "Broken God"]

I disagree that the wording is awkward. The stuff about thaumaturgical models etc is needed to justify the connection to SCP-001, and the structure / word choice is there to support the climax at the end of the description.

That said, that paragraph is the last bit I wrote and the part I feel least confident about. I might rewrite it later. EDIT: This has been done — thanks The Great Hippo and Weryllium for the help :)

The Cogwork Orthodoxy considers the use of electronic devices heresy, so it seems weird that their own scriptures would have them be beneficial to the return of the Broken God.

They don't care for electronics, but the four factors contributing to SCP-4882 apply just as well to mechanical clocks. The difference is that accurate electronic clocks are cheap and ubiquitous, which makes SCP-4882 much more likely to manifest through them.

… it seems beyond improbable that the CotBG is aware of a simple way of rebuilding the Broken God yet there is no incident of a Maxwellist cell attempting it themselves …

Note that the containment breaches we see involve rooms chock full of equipment, that have been running for months without interruption. Perhaps the Maxwellists are so fragmented and under-resourced that none of their attempts are worth talking about.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image Source is here and is in public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this to the tune of the Scooby Doo theme

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last paragraph of the description is really awkwardly phrased. Too many unnecessary words and prhases in what is meant to be a concise description of the anomaly. Also, given that the BG is later referred to as simply SCP-001, it'd probably be better to refer to it as such in the description too. EDIT: Alternatively, since this directly ties into the 001 Proposal, a "001-Apotheosis" event.

The addenda do an excelent job of showing just how serious the threat is, the best example being the number of incidents jumping from 39 to over a hundred in a little over a year, but it seems beyond improbable that the CotBG is aware of a simple way of rebuilding the Broken God yet there is no incident of a Maxwellist cell attempting it themselves, only indications of them introducing the tech into the industry. The Foundation would likely have to several overt attempts at bringing back the BG this way every year. I understand that would go against the spirit of this piece of only using real-life events, but the lack of any reference to such attempts is simply inconceivable. Even if said massive jumps in incidents over the last few years is meant to be Maxwellist attempts, it should be made explicit rather than simply inferred.

A couple of issues with the lore used:

  • The Cogwork Orthodoxy considers the use of electronic devices heresy, so it seems weird that their own scriptures would have them be beneficial to the return of the Broken God.
  • The term "Mekhane" to refer to the Broken God is exclusive to the Cogwork Orthodoxy and maybe the ancient Greek Mekhanites (and in their case it would be an "Allah is just God in Arabic" situation). The Maxwellist refer to it as WAN and the Broken Church has no particular name.

I liked this article, but there's too many problems in it for me to ignore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4882

God damn you. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I like this quite a lot, in fact. One thing I do feel the need to point out, having worked at my country's standards body for a while, is that google's implementation of leap seconds is considered wrong by international standards, and I'm not sure whether the link to their post is meant to imply whether it is the 'correct' implementation in this iteration of the foundationverse or not. Ultimately, it's a minor nitpick, but one that I figured I'd point out. Regardless, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve always hated the idea of Internet of Things. Wiring up your house to a computer that is either a). easily hackable from the internet or b). in controlled by a company with a history of abuse sounds like a very bad idea to me.

This one is just so well put together I’m in awe. The way it links real world events to the Foundation is done spectacularly, and the whole idea is magical on its own merit. My one complaint is that it ends on a weird note, but I can overlook this.

+1

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded:
This is a really well-written SCP! Fantastic use of real events as well. And for your first SCP? Wow.

+1

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best entries I have ever seen. As I read it I thought it must have been made by one of the sites best writers. It is utterly incredible. Well done!

item.title: Author Post!
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP!

In hindsight I should have probably started with a concept less expansive in scope, but ah well.

I'd like to thank these outrageously attractive reviewers:

  • CorpseOfBixby for believing in my idea!
  • qntm for also believing in my idea!
  • anamazingfantasy for critiquing my draft!
  • Pedantique for making me rewrite the whole thing omg

Please tell me what you think!

+ Spoilers
− Spoilers

A heart beats approximately once per second.
